author: @lanceramshur9124
text: I’ve experimented with this very thing before just out of my personal curiosity.  I used a Venturi pump without a check valve in it so as water was running it would pull water out of tank and a one way valve would let air into tank which eliminated the need to have a diaphragm or balloon inside since air was replaced in tank upon any water line being opened.  Then when water was off it would fill tank and compress the air in it.  I 3d printed the tank shape in a all mesh print of petg then coated on a rotating armature like ppl use to resin tumblers/cups.  I never pressure tested this tank and I was using household faucet pressure for my test.  Would make a neat video to see someone pressure test such a tank or even replicate my backwoods little project and maximize its potential.  That would be a hint Mr Smith :)…love your content btw.  If you want my homemade resin tank let me know I will ship it to you or give you print files whatever you need.

author: @patriekbruurs3424
text:
6:00 this is so wrong, you should take a thermodynamics course. You can't store any energy in an incompressible fluid, you can only do this in an compressible fluid or gas and water can in this case be considered as an incompressible fluid. All the energy that is stored in your vessels is stored in the air, not in the water. Btw, that's also why we test burst pressure of vessels when the vessels are filled with water and not with air.
The water in all these examples is used to transfer energy, not to store energy. In the first examples the energy is stored in the weights as potential energy.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: It is my understanding that pressure vessels for water must be designed under particular standards because as the pressure increases the risk of explosion is increased. Additionally, at least part of that reason relates not just to the increased pressure but that water inside these vessels can interact corrosively with the material of the container, so the container itself can't simply be some pressure vessel that used to contain a gas... In the USA, pressure vessels designed to hold water under very high pressures are required by law to meet certain requirements. Likely the cost of such a pressure vessel would outway the long term savings. Additionally, such pressure vessels typically have a regulatory maintenance and inspection requirement, which would almost certainly not be cost effective for most people. However, at lower pressures, we do have "Water Well" Tanks with Air bladders that can be used to pressurize the water. These have a much lower PSI rating, but can safely store water under their rated pressures without as much hassle, but obviously with less stored energy.

author: @jonp3526
text:
Ahoy Robert,
The older (Victorian& Edwardian) accumulators were often incorporated into hydraulic power systems, for example a network to supply power to capstans, cranes and lock gates in a dockyard, presses for sheet metal forming etc... (Also including the power supply of swinging bridges etc...and lifting or bascule bridges...see Tower Bridge as an example)  The "charging" was supplied by steam power pumping water under pressure into the accumulator. As power was drained by whatever machinery was in use the system, once a mechanical trip mechanism had been activated the pump started and re-charged or indeed maintained the pressure in the accumulator. 

So a water accumulator in the old sense makes for an excellent means of storing, controlling and distributing power, but is reliant upon the "battery" being either charged and replenished. The hydraulic engines are normally all reciprocating. These engines also work with a "head" of water being supplied from an elevated source by pipework.   A more measured use of the water results... probably?

author: @dj1NM3
text:
At 04:05 you mention that the pressure in the accumulator is many multiples of mains pressure.
Surely a way to use this higher pressure for a much longer time, would be to use a pressure regulator to drop the accumulator pressure down, to a more manageable near-mains pressure?

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: ( If the Accumulator Pressure is higher than Mains - there is no way the mains pressure is ever going to "budge" the accumulator - volume except underhammer conditions.  The only way to "make "real use" of that higher pressure, is to dump the pressure, batch fill the "water reservoir"  - isolated from the mains, or you are about to evacuate the mains - and re-pressurise the accumulator bladder....  Accumulators in systems are there to make up for pressure and / or flow fluctuations / variations resulting from:  circuit / source switching, pumping pulsation or switching cycles , variable loads in the system - etc..   (Or in the case of a very low power input system - running constantly (high duty cycle) - providing energy for a low duty cycle batch process...).   Air filled vs nitrogen filled...  horses for courses...  Electric systems and batteries are so much more user friendly and load agnostic than these old school industrial systems.

replies.author: @dj1NM3
replies.text:
@kadmow I think you might be making a rash (but incorrect) presumption that the hydraulic accumulator system I described would be connected to the public water main.
I only stated that the output pressure of the accumulator could be regulated down, so that it could be used for a longer period at a lower, but still useful pressure (perhaps spinning a turbine to generate electricity from the stored energy).

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Here is a free idea I have used years ago. In a normal borehole water system you need to have distribution pressure which is achieved in one of 2 ways. Raise the tank say 10 meters or so to get 1 bar or use a distribution pump which has a bladder in it like the one you showed here. Or a third way which is get the 10 000 liter tank back on the ground level and make it strong enough to handle 3 or 4 bar and make it air tight. All that is needed is an inlet pipe with a oneway valve which is connected to the borehole pump in the well and also an outlet pipe at the bottom for feeding the user system.  Now with it being empty at first fill it to say 6000 liters with borehole water. As the water fills up the container it displaces the air which starts to get compressed. You will now have  about 6000 liters with just over 1 bar and with no tower high up and no distribution pump. Much cheaper and easier than the tower or distribution pump. Btw any energy sources could be used for filling the tank, windmill or solar etc be used for the borehole pump.

Thanks for the  concepts - I would love to see a concrete tank explode at 5 bar...   (or piss water at every induced orifice)

replies.text: @kadmow It is actually possible and for a few years worked well until the air  leaks started. Never pushed it beyond that  bar for safety sake but eventually it developed air leaks. Later we realised it needed plastic as well as steel reinforcing in the construction design. The next version will be to take a plastic 10k liter water container and put it in a steel reinforced outer shell.  1 bar is not much pressure in reality. Did you know plastic Coke bottles go beyond 7 bar pressure  which is the max my compressor goes. Did you know that coke is filled to a pressure of 2 bar in those plastic bottles.

author: @klintkrossa6885
text: There is a channle that is land to house. He uses "ram pumps". How would you suggest to use the overflow water for power harvesting?

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: You don't. As a nutural consequence of using it to run the ram pump, it comes out at the lowest elevation you have access to, because that's where you put a ram pump to maximize the velocity of the feed water. The fact that it's coming out at the lowest elevation possible means all of the potential(gravitational) energy in it is spent. It also comes out with a greatly reduced velocity meaning it has much less energy from momentum, and has pulses of 0 current. The energy in it is either spent, or the ram pump is poorly optimized, or if the ram pump does what it needs to without being at the lowest available elevation, then you let the overflow accumulate in a container to even out the pulses and let it drop down to power a turbine.

author: @Nanamowa
text:
I had an idea for an auxilary water suppy that uses check valves on your main to determine where the water you use comes from, then you need only raise the air pressure inside your auxilery tank to the point it's higher than the pressure on the main and that water pressure will for the main check valve closed.

The main purpose of this idea of mine is supplementing my city water with rainwater after making it potable. I'd love to have as many mechanical parts as possible, but it seems unlikely I won't need a small computer to compare the pressure gages and to intermittently release the air pressure so my resevoir can passively fill up with water.

replies.author: @seeker1015
replies.text: Water companies aren't happy with dual systems. If their water is turned off for maintenance, your water may flow back, "polluting" their water. Well, at least that's what they say. Highly unlikely but I get their point. I use isolating valves instead given me definite isolation. Check valves can leak and/or fail.

author: @AverLife
text: Why don't Royal Gases(helium argon neon etc) or air escape into the vacuum of space, as there is no pressurized container? If you say gravity, then how does the gas rise to begin with? Also compare train tankers that have the air pulled out of them(Youtube) and they collapse inward. Just a thought...

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text:
Helium does escape into space. Thats why we're running out of it.
And the answer for the rest is gravity. Light gasses rise because more atomically dense ones displace them. Just like if you take a ball pit for kids and dump metal ball bearings into it, the denser ball bearings will drop through the lighter plastic balls, displacing them and pushing them up and eventually out of the ball pit. Btw, argon sinks in air, and the term is "noble", not "royal".

author: @aaronfranklin324
text:
I have a small waterblaater pump that produces over 2500 psi. Obviously water can be pumped at high pressure far more efficiently than air. Due to its far lower compressibility.
And obviously a jet of water at 300 metres per second for example, carries a lot more kinetic energy than the same volume of air at that speed.
Higher than sonic velocities can be reached with delavel hourglass nozzles with a small hole to introduce atmospheric pressure air near the throat of the Venturi. This enables acceleration out the expansion cone to over 1000 metres per second at water pressure as low as 80 or 100 psi. It of course being a mixture of air bubbles and water then emitted in that jet. 
If your turbine is in fact the expansion cone, is a turbo expander, then you can extract energy and refrigerate simultaneously,  then use a secondary stage to recover the remaining energy. Such as a pelton or hyperbolic Tesla Like Rob has been working on.

author: @seabeepirate
text:
Neat! I’ve seen old accumulators on old buildings. Just now when I saw the weights stacked on top the water it made me wonder if a water tower could have a double accumulation action by making the water tank slide up and down rails so that it’s own weight is supported by the water pressure and would have a piston effect on the mains pressure. For those who don’t know, the weight of the water in the bulb/tank at the top of the tower does not contribute pressure laterally, only vertically. That weight is supported solely by the ground underneath. A narrow vertical pipe would produce the same pressure as a tower with a tank at the top, but it would not be as steady as the bulb/tank because the bulb shape acts as a buffer to the vertical stacking water that produces/maintains pressure in the lines. 

Don’t turbines all use pressure? Some convert high pressure with high momentum and some use low pressure with mechanical advantage to concentrate momentum but they all convert pressure as a result of momentum into mechanical movement right? Maybe I’ve assigned some attributes to the wrong factors.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
- weelll.... the pelton turbine actually uses kinetic energy - as it turns this into impulse (the pressure is released at the jet - it goes to zero as the velocity approaches maximum) to create useful work..

Reaction turbines use bulk flow creating torque, again the  pressure is being traded for speed - the conversions are optimised for what is available - and what you can (want) to get out.) - stored water is Only useful  (apart from drinking, industry and agriculture) in terms of energy by value of the Mass and the "pressure head" (potential energy) - the more "potential energy, the less mass must be traded for a "giiven" amount of energy - how fast it is released influences the "energy rate - or power.

(all these arguments  - points for discussion/  optimisation - can help demonstrate why sometimes (compare turbo generators to Aircraft propulsion) a propeller is the best option, othertimes a "jet" is best - -while for an in between "gulf" a mixture between gives a more optimal outcome - ie a pressure optimised  Francis runner.) - google has many sources for all questions.

author: @rfiskillingussoftly6568
text: Great video man!

author: @jajwarehouse1
text: The accumulator not only acts as a battery, but also functions like a capacitor for smoothing the outgoing pressure over time.  Single piston pumps act like AC current with the pressure high and low as the piston alternates direction.  Triple piston pumps act more like 3-phase current, however the accumulator still helps when the pump rate is not consistent due to the initial energy supply changing, such as the windmill or rain slowing down.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: ( do you mean a big pipe running up a hill/?? - (at the same pressure, all pumping is similar)  one could also fill a "cablecar" /elevated tramway, with - (water) and tow it up a mountain - all examples of gravity storage - the pipe running up a hill to a "tank" (continuous process is a lot more "scalable" than the cablecar (batch process) - but either is not wrong depending on the metrics.  (Train vs Conveyor belt = bulk transport optimisation problem) )

replies.author: @isaacm1929
replies.text:
@kadmow The battery would be like a normal pipe (but BIG, in length AND diameter) with the side UPHILL being the input for the water, and the part DOWNHILL a normal pipe (SMALL, in length and diameter) with one side open to the GENERATOR (which is a normal water generator)
Basically, imagine the test he made in his video with a syringe and a needle, but WITHOUT the plunger.
The pumps would be like a normal water gravity storage, just "ON" all the time, because when the supply of energy couldn't put more water IN than it goes OUT, you'll be in a moment where you need to use the battery, making it also have a "gravity switch". Also, having it "ON" all the time scavenges energy even when using the battery. 
Basically, (IF INPUT > OUTPUT = you have energy on the system, and most is coming for the outer generators) and (IF INPUT < OUTPUT = you have energy on the system, and most is the battery spending what it stored)

author: @wr6293
text:
From my experience with industrial hydraulic accumulators I am a bit skeptical about them being used as kinda battery storage. Besides the need for a high pressure pump these there is pressure bleeding as no valve is 100 per cent leak free. Means over time the pressure drops and you need to top up for that lost pressure.
In addition I am not keen of having a pressure system with several hundred bars at home and it piping even if it would be in an individual room. Any small leak will discharge a stream of water like they are used in water cutters to cut 100 mm steel plates. Even in an industrial setting a pressure below 130 bar is preferred as the technical and regulatory requirements are much lower at that level compared with pressure above that line.
Not saying it could not be done but in my opinion there are less difficult solutions available. But that is just my opinion

replies.author: @adymode
replies.text: The idea is basically same as familiar compressed gas power sources, the added complexity of water being involved as a medium and adding a little extra gravity storage lets imaginations run with the idea.

replies.author: @wr6293
replies.text:
@adymode As rule of thumb each 10 m height adds 1 bar. So for 100 bar you just need to put your water tank 1000 m above the level of your turbine…. Yeah, that is surely more practical than the usual water storage in big reservoirs using huge pipe diameter and flow of water to drive turbines.
There are certainly ways of using water as source of energy like small creeks but compressed water imho is not for energy storage. And as you wrote using water instead of gases adds a level of complexity - both in the mechanics and safety aspect - that is not really worth it.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: In South Australia there was.mention of storing compressed gas at say 2 or 3 hundred metres lower underwater in bags anchored to the bottom. A vague recollection though .

replies.author: @wr6293
@adymode
replies.text: The energy stored in the situation which Robert described is simply the energy in the raised water plus the energy spent compressing a bladder. The suggestion has been that the two add up to more than the sum, but they obviously dont. The bladder depressurizes as the water squirts out and if you store too much energy in the bladder it just bursts. The use of water doesnt multiply the storage components, they just add together. There is no breakthrough or untried invention involved, there is just an absence of rudimentry engineering formulation.

author: @Arturo-lapaz
text:
With all due respect, the use of a Pelton wheel has the highest efficiency overall. 
But to achieve this efficiency the pressure of the water must be constant. How? The turbine drives a generator for 60 herz AC, implilying that the jet speed is exactly twice the cup speed, so that the efflux drops out laterally. with essential zero kinetic energy.
The water pressure must be such that the velocity V is
V = √( 2 × g × h) 
   = √(2  press / density)
V = Pelton wheel radius rpm×2 π /60
rpm = 60 n/ polenumber
n = 60 Hertz   in the US,  
       50 Hertz in Europe
h is the height of the lake over the jet nozzle in a pumped hydro case
When you use continiously sinking pressure 
Any pressure above nominal must be throttled to achieve this constant jet velocity, 
Below, the jet is useless.
Using an active control system .
Throttling is highly in efficient.
The pressurized constant volume storage system is terribly inefficient, sorry to say

text: F  = MV^2.  By accelerating the water using a pressure vessel, the available power goes up by the square of the exit velocity. Gotta love Isaac Newton…

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
(f  = ma     = m v' (acceleration being the first time derivative of velocity - second time derivative of position (ie x''), not the square..)

Kinetic Energy = 1/2 m*v^2  (the integral of momentum  momentum = m*V)

Power = Force * Velocity = m*a*v = m*a*(x/t)

replies.author: @shinjisan2015
replies.text: Yeah I also had to think about it a bit more to plot the whole process and figure out how it's actually storing and then recovering energy. So you have an accumulator pressure vessel. The vessel is pressurised using compressed air. Inside the vessel is a bladder filled with water. The volume of compressed air surrounds the water bladder. The bladder is filled using a water pump that is powered by solar/wind. When there is no solar/wind, the water in the bladder is released. The pressurised air on the outside of the bladder will squeeze the water out of the bladder. The water is sprayed out a nozzle to increase pressure (Bernoulli's principle) as demonstrated by the syringe. That nozzle sprays onto a generator and walah, recovered energy from the "battery". :)

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: @shinjisan2015  - and all that energy now has to be re-put-back-in before the next shot happens...  (the recharge stage was mentioned but not demonstrated._

author: @Handy-Dandy-Dave
text: As we talk about free energy, how about the ram pump? You can pump it up hill and maybe put it in a water tower, and then it can be put in a pipe down the same hill to a generator, and then you collect the same water down to the same ram pump? You would have to put some water back into the system, but very little. It would be an ever running system! Foremost, I don't have a place of my own to be able to do that. Not everyone can do it! But for the ones that can, it's endless possibility. Just a thought! Of course, it is not the simple thing to do.

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: Ram pumps aren't magic. A large volume of water is wasted to pump a small volume at higher pressure. There's no way to recycle  the pumped water through the ram pump and retain even a majority fraction, much less most of it, much much less almost all of it.

author: @roostershangarworkshop8051
text: You could maybe also increase the water pressure by adding a heating element outside the tank (outside to avoid compromising the pressure integrity) and boiling the water. Heating elements can work off of direct solar so no batteries required. You may even be able to drive your generator off of steam instead of pressurized water. Obviously you're going to need some pressure relief valves, but you could basically make a little steam generator out of those pressure containers. You could combine this with your carbon fiber heating elements which you describe how to make in previous videos.

text: Why does a ram pump come to mind?

replies.author: @LES_Thermodynamics
replies.text: Agreed! The compressed air from the ram pump could pressurize a bladder inside of a water tank

replies.author: @jamesgribben7024
replies.text: Cause they usually have accumulators to level out pressure. A ram pump can consist of only spring valves and pipe. The accumulator reduces wear on valve

replies.author: @jswets5007
replies.text: ​@jamesgribben7024 You can also use the flow of the water powering the ram pump to fill a second accumulator without adding any more energy to the system. Then you can use the pressure from the second to assist the water pressure in the same or another system. Of course it will never create more energy than is used to initiate the system(s), but it could potentially improve the efficiency of them.

author: @aaronsj80
text: I have a pressure tank on my well. I never realized they had balloons in them. I'm guessing that's so the pressure tank can work in any orientation whether upside down or upright.

replies.author: @chrisroberts7573
replies.text: It's to stop a phenomenon know as water hammer effect as well as storing an amount of pressurised water so your pump doesn't need to turn on every time the tap is turned on

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: A bladder is used because presurized air will dissolve into wate and be carried away into your pipes, then released when you open a tap. The end result of this would be that all of the air in tank would vanish, it would be entirely water locked, and there would be no buffer in the system. Thia would mean that the tiniest leak would cause your pump to kick on and almost instantly hit peak pressure, over and over. This would result in the pump dying much faster.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
While I do not know exactly how much your water costs in a city water system anywhere in the world [I live on a farm and have a well pump] I have to wonder if just having a positive displacement vane in the flow of your home water inlet, perhaps you might scavenge a little bit of electrical energy, just from your daily water use.

While the power is not 'free' because one is paying for the water being sent to their home, if one could just use the otherwise totally unused potential energy, it SHOULD make no difference to their actual cost whatsoever, nor should it really restrict the flow of the water, as it is going to be moving either way, only in this case, with a positive displacement vane stator, is that one could turn a little generator with that pressure as it makes its way on toward and out of your tap.
